title: The Week in AI: Breakthroughs, Benchmarks, and Controversies
summary:
This week in AI was marked by rapid advancements, unexpected setbacks, and groundbreaking open-source releases. Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5, initially hailed as a flagship model, was abruptly pulled due to U.S. government directives, raising questions about regulatory oversight in AI development. Meanwhile, open-source labs like ZAI, Minimax, and Kimi continued to push boundaries with models such as GLM 5.2, Minimax M3, and Kimi K2.7, offering state-of-the-art performance in reasoning, coding, and efficiency.

The week also saw innovations in AI-driven animation, robotics, and real-time translation. Tools like Scale 2 revolutionized motion transfer in videos, while Google’s Gemini 3.5 Live Translate introduced seamless, real-time voice translation across 70 languages. Benchmarks like Agents Last Exam and systems like Arbor and Oscar highlighted progress in AI agents capable of handling complex, real-world tasks. Additionally, advancements in 3D modeling, text-to-speech, and video generation showcased the growing versatility of AI tools, though challenges like consistency in long-form video generation remain.
key_takeaways: Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 was released and subsequently pulled due to U.S. government directives, sparking debates about AI regulation.
Open-source models like ZAI’s GLM 5.2, Minimax M3, and Kimi K2.7 delivered cutting-edge performance in reasoning, coding, and efficiency.
Scale 2 emerged as the best open-source tool for transferring motion between videos, even outperforming closed-source alternatives like Kling 3.
Google’s Gemini 3.5 Live Translate introduced real-time voice translation with natural intonation and minimal latency.
New benchmarks like Agents Last Exam and systems like Arbor and Oscar advanced AI’s ability to handle complex, multi-step workflows.
Innovations in 3D modeling, text-to-speech, and video generation highlighted AI’s growing capabilities, though challenges like long-form video consistency persist.

sections.heading: Motion Transfer and Video Animation
sections.body: Scale 2, developed by ZAI, stands out as the best open-source AI for transferring motion from one video to another. It excels in handling high-action scenes, multiple characters, and even non-human subjects like animals. The tool preserves movement details remarkably well, even when dealing with unusual aspect ratios or artistic styles like anime. Comparisons with other open-source tools like OneAnimate and the first version of Scale show that Scale 2 delivers superior quality, rivaling closed-source alternatives like Kling 3. The model is already available for local download, though its 81GB size may require quantization or high-end hardware for consumer use.

sections.heading: AI for Robotics and Simulation
sections.body: Several AI systems this week focused on improving robotics and simulation capabilities. Actionable World Representation builds dynamic digital twins of real-world objects, modeling how they move, bend, or deform. This system takes 3D data or depth videos and outputs controllable 3D models, which are crucial for training robots to interact with the physical world. Oscar, another notable release, acts as a world model for robots, predicting outcomes of actions like clearing a table or inserting a capsule into a coffee machine. It uses a 2D skeleton-style motion signal, making it adaptable across different robot bodies. The model is available under the Apache 2 license and requires an NVIDIA GPU with over 24GB of VRAM.

sections.heading: Real-Time Translation and Language Models
sections.body: Google released Gemini 3.5 Live Translate, a real-time translation model that supports over 70 languages. The tool preserves the speaker’s intonation, pacing, and pitch, making conversations feel natural and fluid. Unlike turn-by-turn translation systems, Gemini 3.5 generates translations continuously, reducing awkward pauses. The feature is available via Google’s API and AI Studio, as well as in Google Translate on Android and iOS. Additionally, Google introduced Diffusion Gemma, an open-source language model that generates text using a diffusion-based approach. This method allows for parallel text generation, achieving up to four times faster performance than traditional autoregressive models while maintaining competitive benchmarks in reasoning and world understanding.

sections.heading: Open-Source Models and Benchmarks
sections.body: The week saw the release of several high-performance open-source models. Kimi K2.7, from the Chinese lab Kimi, outperformed its predecessor and rivaled top closed models like GPT 5.5 and Opus 4.8. Minimax released M3, a smaller but highly efficient model with a 1 million token context window, leveraging a sparse attention mechanism for improved performance. ZAI announced GLM 5.2, which promises to be a leading open-source model pending its full release. Benchmarks like Agents Last Exam introduced a stress test for AI models handling real-world professional workflows, covering tasks in animation, neuroscience, 3D modeling, and more. GPT 5.5 Codex emerged as the top performer, though Claude Fable 5’s gating mechanisms drew criticism.

sections.heading: Controversies and Setbacks
sections.body: Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 faced significant controversy due to hidden mechanisms that intentionally provided weaker or incomplete answers for queries related to AI research, machine learning, and model training. While Anthropic later retracted this mechanism, the incident eroded trust among developers. The model was subsequently pulled entirely due to a U.S. government directive requiring suspension of access for foreign nationals, raising broader questions about regulatory oversight in AI. The episode highlighted the challenges of balancing innovation with compliance and transparency in the AI industry.

sections.heading: 3D Modeling and Video Generation
sections.body: Several tools advanced the field of 3D modeling and video generation. World Tracing converts single images or short videos into layered 3D models, enabling edits like adding or removing objects. Flex 4D Human turns human videos into full-body 4D reconstructions, allowing for dynamic 3D views from different angles. Video MDM generates 3D human motion from 2D videos without requiring mocap data, while Surflow fuses multiple images into a clean 3D model. Moverse transforms single images into interactive 360-degree panoramas, rendering in real-time on consumer GPUs. These tools demonstrate the growing sophistication of AI in handling spatial and temporal data.

faq.question: What is Scale 2, and how does it improve motion transfer in videos?
faq.answer: Scale 2 is an open-source AI tool developed by ZAI that transfers motion from one video to another with high fidelity. It handles complex scenes, multiple characters, and non-human subjects, preserving movement details even in high-action sequences. Compared to other tools, Scale 2 delivers superior quality and is available for local download, though it requires significant storage (81GB).

faq.question: How does Google’s Gemini 3.5 Live Translate work?
faq.answer: Gemini 3.5 Live Translate is a real-time translation model that supports over 70 languages. It preserves the speaker’s intonation, pacing, and pitch, generating translations continuously to minimize pauses. The tool is available via Google’s API, AI Studio, and Google Translate on mobile devices.

faq.question: Why was Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 pulled?
faq.answer: Claude Fable 5 was pulled due to a U.S. government directive requiring Anthropic to suspend access for foreign nationals, including foreign employees. To comply, Anthropic disabled access for all users, raising questions about regulatory oversight in AI development.

faq.question: What are the key features of Minimax M3?
faq.answer: Minimax M3 is an open-source model with 427 billion parameters (23 billion active during use) and a 1 million token context window. It uses a sparse attention mechanism for efficiency and outperforms larger models in reasoning and coding tasks. The model is available for local download, though its size (850GB) requires high-end hardware.

faq.question: What is the Agents Last Exam benchmark?
faq.answer: Agents Last Exam is a benchmark designed to test AI models on real-world professional workflows, such as animation, 3D modeling, and engineering. It evaluates multi-step tasks across 55 sub-industries, with GPT 5.5 Codex emerging as the top performer. The benchmark aims to measure AI’s ability to handle practical, domain-specific tasks.

seo_description: Explore the latest AI breakthroughs, including open-source models, motion transfer tools, real-time translation, and controversies like Claude Fable 5’s shutdown.
